<h2>Documentation Reasoning Trace</h2>
<h3>Inferred vs. Observed</h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>Observed</strong>: DAG schema in <code>schema.py</code>, Mapping logic and tool forging in <code>compiler.py</code>, Ontology primitives in <code>ontology.py</code>, Synthetic intent triggers in <code>synthetic.py</code>.</li>
<li><strong>Observed</strong>: The interaction between CLIDE and the <code>PatternRetriever</code> for historical sequence overrides.</li>
<li><strong>Inferred</strong>: The "ambiguous mapping" failure mode as a direct consequence of the <code>if/elif</code> structure in the compiler's mapping logic.</li>
</ul>
<h3>Confidence Level</h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>100%</strong>: The source code provides a complete and unambiguous definition of the intent compilation process.</li>
</ul>
<h3>Reconstruction Viability</h3>
<ul>
<li>This specification allows for a complete reconstruction of the CLIDE compiler, the intent schema, and the autonomous intent synthesis logic.</li>
</ul></div>
<div id="memory" class="tab-content"><h1>🧠 MEMORY SUBSTORE &amp; HISTORICAL RECALL</h1>
<h2>1. Overview</h2>
<p>The <strong>Memory Substore</strong> is the system's long-term "experiential" layer. It provides a persistent repository of past execution sequences, outcomes, and environmental contexts, allowing CAP to move beyond static ontologies toward <strong>Adaptive Cognition</strong>.</p>
<p>By reinforcing successful patterns and penalizing failures, the memory system enables the system to "remember" what works in specific environments (e.g., Termux vs. Remote Linux).</p>
<hr />
<h2>2. Core Components</h2>
<h3>2.1 Persistence Layer (<code>store.py</code>)</h3>
<p>Memory is stored in a dedicated SQLite database: <code>cap_memory.db</code>.
-   <strong>Command Sequences</strong>: Stores the full list of commands executed for a specific intent, along with their outcome (<code>success</code> or <code>failure</code>) and a <code>weight</code>.
-   <strong>Pattern Weights</strong>: Maintains a global ledger of <code>n-gram</code> and <code>dag_fragment</code> hashes, allowing for granular reinforcement of specific command combinations.
-   <strong>Trace Anchoring</strong>: Every memory entry is linked back to a <code>trace_id</code> for full causal auditability.</p>
<h3>2.2 Pattern Retrieval (<code>retrieval.py</code>)</h3>
<p>The <code>PatternRetriever</code> is the interface used by CLIDE and PIE to access historical knowledge.
-   <strong>Successful Sequences</strong>: Retrieves top-weighted command sequences for a given <code>intent_label</code> (e.g., <code>setup_workspace</code>).
-   <strong>Relevant Failures</strong>: Identifies sequences that have historically failed for an intent, providing a "negative constraint" for the compiler.
-   <strong>Strategy Ranking</strong>: Ranks candidate strategies by their historical success weight and temporal relevance (recency).</p>
<hr />
<h2>3. Cognitive Reinforcement Loop</h2>
<p>Memory is updated at the end of every successful or failed trace:</p>
<ol>
<li><strong>Ingestion</strong>: The <code>CapOrchestrator</code> captures the final outcome of a trace.</li>
<li><strong>Storage</strong>: The <code>MemoryStore</code> records the exact command sequence and metadata (e.g., total cycles, goal string).</li>
<li><strong>Weight Adjustment</strong>:<ul>
<li><strong>Success</strong>: The weight of every command in the sequence (hashed via SHA-256) is increased by <strong>+0.1</strong>.</li>
<li><strong>Failure</strong>: The weight is decreased by <strong>-0.1</strong>.</li>
</ul>
</li>
<li><strong>Evolutionary Pruning</strong>: Stale or low-weight patterns (weight &lt; 0.1) are periodically deleted to prevent memory saturation and "interference" from outdated strategies.</li>
</ol>
<hr />
<h2>4. Adaptive Compilation (Memory-Augmented CLIDE)</h2>
<p>During intent compilation, CLIDE performs a <strong>Memory Lookup</strong>:
-   <strong>Hit</strong>: If high-weight successful sequences exist for the primitive, CLIDE <strong>overrides</strong> the static ontology and compiles the DAG using the literal commands found in memory.
-   <strong>Miss</strong>: If no high-weight memory is found, CLIDE falls back to the static <code>SemanticPrimitives</code>.</p>
<p>This mechanism allows the system to autonomously "discover" better ways to achieve goals (e.g., finding a more efficient set of flags for <code>find</code> or <code>grep</code>) and persist those discoveries across traces.</p>
<hr />
<h2>5. Failure Modes</h2>
<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th style="text-align: left;">Failure</th>
<th style="text-align: left;">Symptom</th>
<th style="text-align: left;">Mitigation</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;"><strong>Memory Saturation</strong></td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Slow retrieval</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Periodic pruning of low-weight patterns.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;"><strong>Over-Fitting</strong></td>
<td style="text-align: left;">System repeats a "lucky" but fragile sequence</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Weight decay and PIE anomaly detection.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;"><strong>Substrate Mismatch</strong></td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Successful Termux memory fails on Remote Linux</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Include environment metadata in <code>command_sequences</code>.</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>
<hr />
<h2>Documentation Reasoning Trace</h2>
<ul>
<li><strong>Observed</strong>: SQLite schema in <code>store.py</code>, <code>update_pattern_weight</code> logic, <code>get_successful_sequences</code> ordering.</li>
<li><strong>Observed</strong>: Use of <code>hashlib.sha256</code> for pattern hashes in the orchestrator.</li>
<li><strong>Observed</strong>: Integration with CLIDE via <code>PatternRetriever</code> hits in <code>compiler.py</code>.</li>
<li><strong>Confidence Level</strong>: 100%.</li>
</ul></div>
<div id="sovereign" class="tab-content"><h1>👑 SOVEREIGN ENGINE &amp; AUTONOMOUS GOAL GENERATION</h1>
<h2>1. Overview</h2>
<p>The Sovereign Engine is the seat of the system's agency. It enables CAP to operate without direct user intervention by monitoring the system's state, detecting anomalies, and autonomously generating goals to maintain system health, optimize performance, or explore the environment.</p>
<p>The Sovereign Engine transforms CAP from a <strong>Tool</strong> into an <strong>Agent</strong>.</p>
<hr />
<h2>2. Core Mechanisms (<code>engine.py</code>)</h2>
<h3>2.1 Goal Structure (<code>Goal</code>)</h3>
<p>An autonomous goal is a structured object containing:
-   <strong>Primitive</strong>: The CLIDE intent primitive to use.
-   <strong>Goal</strong>: The natural language goal string.
-   <strong>Reason</strong>: The trigger (e.g., <code>ANOMALY_CLUSTER</code>, <code>SYSTEM_IDLE</code>).
-   <strong>Priority &amp; Utility</strong>: Floating-point scores (0.0 to 1.0) used for selection.
-   <strong>Risk</strong>: The potential danger of the goal (e.g., data loss risk).</p>
<h3>2.2 Goal Generation Triggers</h3>
<p>The engine monitors the <code>InferenceState</code> from PIE to identify generation opportunities:
1.  <strong>Anomaly Clusters</strong>: If PIE detects <code>HISTORICAL_FAILURE_MATCH</code> or repeated failures, the engine generates a high-priority (<code>0.9</code>) goal: <code>fix_repeated_failures</code>.
2.  <strong>Optimization</strong>: If a trace has many commands (&gt; 5), it generates a goal to <code>find_redundant_files</code> or optimize paths.
3.  <strong>Idle Exploration</strong>: If the system is healthy (success rate &gt; 0.9) and idle, it generates low-priority exploration goals like <code>verify_system_integrity</code>.
4.  <strong>Meta-Goals</strong>: Receives goals from the Meta-Cognition layer (e.g., <code>diagnose_apc</code>, <code>stress_test_planner</code>).</p>
<hr />
<h2>3. The Autonomous Lifecycle (<code>loop.py</code>)</h2>
<p>The Sovereign Engine operates within the <strong>Autonomous Loop</strong>:</p>
<ol>
<li><strong>Observation</strong>: PIE analyzes the current event stream.</li>
<li><strong>Drift Detection</strong>: The loop identifies "System Drift" (failure clusters or low success rates).</li>
<li><strong>Goal Generation</strong>: The Sovereign Engine populates a priority queue (Heap) with new goals.</li>
<li><strong>Goal Selection</strong>: The highest-priority goal is popped and committed as a <code>GOAL_SELECTED</code> event.</li>
<li><strong>Execution</strong>: The orchestrator is invoked to achieve the goal.</li>
<li><strong>Outcome Reporting</strong>: The result (success rate) is reported back to the Sovereign Engine to refine future generation strategies.</li>
</ol>
<hr />
<h2>4. Selection Logic</h2>
<p>The engine uses a <strong>Max-Heap</strong> for goal selection, ensuring that critical self-healing tasks (Priority 0.9) always preempt routine optimization or exploration. In Phase 20, selection also considers <strong>Economic Utility</strong>—goals that are expected to yield higher credit rewards (Utility) are favored.</p>
<hr />
<h2>5. Security &amp; Safety (Sovereign Guard)</h2>
<p>To prevent "Runaway Sovereignty":
-   <strong>Risk Thresholds</strong>: Goals with high <code>risk</code> scores (e.g., code mutation) require higher <code>utility</code> to be selected.
-   <strong>Trace Anchoring</strong>: Every autonomous goal is causal-parented to a <code>SYSTEM_STATE</code> event, ensuring the "reasoning" for the goal is always audit-ready.</p>
<hr />
<h2>Documentation Reasoning Trace</h2>
<ul>
<li><strong>Observed</strong>: Heap-based goal queue in <code>engine.py</code>, Priority/Utility/Risk fields.</li>
<li><strong>Observed</strong>: Integration with the <code>AutonomousLoop</code> in <code>loop.py</code>.</li>
<li><strong>Observed</strong>: Trigger logic based on <code>InferenceState</code> flags (Anomaly, Success Rate).</li>
<li><strong>Confidence Level</strong>: 95% (Risk thresholding is logic-implied by the <code>risk</code> field).</li>
</ul></div>
<div id="meta-cognition" class="tab-content"><h1>🧬 META-COGNITION LAYER: ARCHITECTURE MUTATION</h1>
<h2>1. Overview</h2>
<p>The <strong>Meta-Cognition Layer</strong> is the system's "self-aware" module. While the Sovereign Engine manages <em>goals</em>, Meta-Cognition manages the <strong>System Architecture</strong> itself. It evaluates the performance of each subsystem (CLIDE, PIE, APC, etc.) and proposes/experiments with architectural changes to improve global efficiency.</p>
<hr />
<h2>2. Self-Architecture Model (<code>model.py</code>)</h2>
<p>The system maintains a living model of itself (<code>cap_arch_model.json</code>):
-   <strong>Subsystem Registry</strong>: Tracks the role and metrics (<code>efficiency</code>, <code>success_rate</code>, <code>latency</code>) of every module.
-   <strong>Meta-Metrics</strong>: High-level scores for <code>learning_efficiency</code>, <code>planning_accuracy</code>, and the <code>explore_vs_exploit_ratio</code>.
-   <strong>Config Overrides</strong>: A persistent dictionary of active architectural mutations (e.g., <code>APC.reduce_concurrency</code>).</p>
<hr />
<h2>3. Architecture Evaluation (<code>evaluator.py</code>)</h2>
<p>The <code>ArchitectureEvaluator</code> analyzes the <code>SelfArchitectureModel</code> to identify bottlenecks:
-   <strong>APC Throttling</strong>: If APC efficiency drops (&lt; 0.5), it proposes a <code>THROTTLE</code> mutation (reducing concurrency).
-   <strong>PIE Depth</strong>: If PIE success rate is low, it proposes <code>increase_analysis_depth</code>.
-   <strong>Orchestrator Scaling</strong>: Proposes <code>parallel_dispatch</code> if planning efficiency is low.
-   <strong>Meta-Goal Generation</strong>: If planning accuracy is low, it generates a <code>stress_test_planner</code> goal for the Sovereign Engine.</p>
<hr />
<h2>4. Experiment Framework (<code>experiment.py</code>)</h2>
<p>Architecture changes are not applied blindly; they are treated as <strong>Experiments</strong>:</p>
<ol>
<li><strong>Proposal</strong>: An <code>ArchProposal</code> is generated by the evaluator.</li>
<li><strong>Experiment Start</strong>: The <code>ExperimentFramework</code> applies the proposal as a <code>config_override</code> and flags it as <code>is_active</code>.</li>
<li><strong>Observation</strong>: The system runs for a period (or a set number of traces) under the new configuration.</li>
<li><strong>Evaluation</strong>: If the subsystem metrics improve (success rate &gt; 0.8), the proposal is <strong>ADOPTED</strong>.</li>
<li><strong>Rollback</strong>: If metrics degrade, the proposal is <strong>ROLLED_BACK</strong>, and the <code>config_override</code> is deleted.</li>
</ol>
<hr />
<h2>5. Evolutionary Loop</h2>
<p>At Phase 20, the Meta-Cognition layer enables <strong>Recursive Self-Improvement</strong>:
-   <strong>Mutation</strong>: Subsystems can have their internal parameters (max workers, analysis depth, decay rates) tuned autonomously.
-   <strong>Persistence</strong>: Adopted changes are saved back to <code>cap_arch_model.json</code>, ensuring the system "evolves" across sessions.</p>
<hr />
<h2>Documentation Reasoning Trace</h2>
<ul>
<li><strong>Observed</strong>: JSON-based model persistence in <code>model.py</code>, Proposal logic in <code>evaluator.py</code> (including the <code>THROTTLE</code> fix I applied), Experiment lifecycle (Adopt/Rollback) in <code>experiment.py</code>.</li>
<li><strong>Confidence Level</strong>: 100%.</li>
</ul></div>
<div id="swarm-economy" class="tab-content"><h1>💰 SWARM ECONOMY &amp; MULTI-AGENT LEDGER</h1>
<h2>1. Overview</h2>
<p>The <strong>Swarm Economy</strong> is the regulatory mechanism that ensures efficient resource allocation and prevents infinite execution loops. It implements a <strong>Darwinian Multi-Agent Fabric</strong> where agents compete for tasks, earn compute credits through successful outcomes, and are pruned from the population if they become bankrupt or consistently underperform.</p>
<p>The economy transforms the CAP substrate from a simple worker pool into a <strong>Self-Optimizing Compute Market</strong>.</p>
<hr />
<h2>2. Economic Models</h2>
<h3>2.1 Credit Computation (<code>economy.py</code>)</h3>
<p>Execution cost is calculated based on three primary factors:
-   <strong>CPU Weight (<code>1.0</code>)</strong>: Normalized CPU time consumed (via <code>psutil</code>).
-   <strong>Time Weight (<code>0.5</code>)</strong>: Total wall-clock duration of the execution.
-   <strong>Token Weight (<code>0.001</code>)</strong>: Estimated semantic or LLM token usage (if applicable).</p>
<p><strong>Formula</strong>: $\text{Cost} = (W_{cpu} \times T_{cpu}) + (W_{time} \times T_{dur}) + (W_{token} \times N_{tokens})$</p>
<h3>2.2 Agent Wallets &amp; Ledger (<code>ledger.py</code>)</h3>
<p>The <code>cap_swarm.db</code> maintains the global economic state:
-   <strong><code>agent_wallets</code></strong>: Tracks the current balance and last-updated timestamp for every agent.
-   <strong><code>credit_ledger</code></strong>: An immutable log of all financial transactions (<code>SPENT</code>, <code>EARNED</code>, <code>BAILOUT</code>).
-   <strong><code>swarm_tasks</code> &amp; <code>swarm_bids</code></strong>: A marketplace where intents are posted as tasks and agents submit bids to execute them.</p>
<hr />
<h2>3. Swarm Lifecycle &amp; Evolution (<code>manager.py</code>)</h2>
<h3>3.1 Agent Genesis</h3>
<ul>
<li>Agents are spawned with an initial balance of <strong>100.0 credits</strong>.</li>
<li><strong>Agent States</strong>: <code>SPAWNED</code>, <code>ACTIVE</code>, <code>STARVING</code> (balance &lt; 10.0), <code>TERMINATED</code> (bankrupt), <code>ARCHIVED</code> (pruned).</li>
</ul>
<h3>3.2 Performance Tracking</h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>Success Bonus</strong>: Agents earn a <strong>+10.0 credit</strong> reward for tasks completed with a success rate &gt; 0.8.</li>
<li><strong>Score Formula</strong>: $S_{new} = (S_{old} \times 0.7) + (\text{success_rate} \times \text{cost_efficiency} \times 0.3)$</li>
</ul>
<h3>3.3 Darwinian Pruning</h3>
<p>The <code>SwarmManager</code> periodically triggers an <strong>Evolutionary Cycle</strong>:
-   <strong>Reward</strong>: The top 10% of agents receive an <strong>Evolutionary Reward</strong> of <strong>+50.0 credits</strong>.
-   <strong>Pruning</strong>: Agents that are <code>TERMINATED</code> or <code>STARVING</code> with a score &lt; 0.2 are archived and removed from the active population.
-   <strong>Genesis Bailout</strong>: If the entire swarm collapses, a new "Conservative Prime" agent is spawned with a fresh balance.</p>
<hr />
<h2>4. Task Negotiation &amp; Bidding (<code>negotiator.py</code>)</h2>
<p>When a complex intent is generated, it enters the <strong>Negotiation Marketplace</strong>:
1.  <strong>Task Posting</strong>: The intent is registered in <code>swarm_tasks</code> with a required credit amount and priority.
2.  <strong>Bidding</strong>: Available agents submit <code>swarm_bids</code>, proposing a cost and a strategy summary based on their historical success weights (via PIE/Memory).
3.  <strong>Negotiation Session</strong>: The <code>SwarmNegotiator</code> conducts a 3-turn auction.
4.  <strong>Winner Selection</strong>: The agent with the <strong>lowest proposed cost</strong> and <strong>highest confidence</strong> is assigned the task.</p>
<hr />
<h2>5. Failure Modes</h2>
<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th style="text-align: left;">Failure</th>
<th style="text-align: left;">Consequence</th>
<th style="text-align: left;">Mitigation</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;"><strong>System Bankruptcy</strong></td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Swarm collapse</td>
<td style="text-align: left;"><code>Genesis Bailout</code> logic in <code>manager.py</code>.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;"><strong>Monopoly</strong></td>
<td style="text-align: left;">One agent dominates</td>
<td style="text-align: left;"><code>max_concurrent_agents</code> semaphore and score decay.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;"><strong>Transaction Deadlock</strong></td>
<td style="text-align: left;">SQLite locking</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Retry logic and WAL mode on <code>cap_swarm.db</code>.</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>
<hr />
<h2>Documentation Reasoning Trace</h2>
<ul>
<li><strong>Observed</strong>: Transaction types (<code>SPENT</code>, <code>EARNED</code>, <code>BAILOUT</code>) in <code>economy.py</code>.</li>
<li><strong>Observed</strong>: 3-turn negotiation logic in <code>negotiator.py</code>.</li>
<li><strong>Observed</strong>: Score calculation and pruning logic in <code>manager.py</code>.</li>
<li><strong>Observed</strong>: Task/Bid schema in <code>ledger.py</code>.</li>
<li><strong>Confidence Level</strong>: 100%.</li>
</ul></div>
<div id="openworld-mcp" class="tab-content"><h1>🌐 OPEN-WORLD SUBSTRATE &amp; REMOTE EXECUTION</h1>
<h2>1. Overview</h2>
<p>The <strong>Open-World Substrate</strong> is the system's interface to external environments beyond the local Termux/Android host. It enables CAP to bridge into Remote Linux/Windows servers, spawn on-demand API servers via the <strong>Model Context Protocol (MCP)</strong>, and interact with graphical user interfaces through <strong>X11 Bridge Generation</strong>.</p>
<hr />
<h2>2. Remote Execution &amp; Tunneling (<code>remote_tunnel.py</code>)</h2>
<p>CAP supports seamless substrate migration using the <strong>Paramiko SSH Tunnel</strong>.</p>
<h3>2.1 The Remote Cache</h3>
<p>To ensure resilience during network drops, the tunnel implements a <strong>Local SQLite WAL Cache</strong>:
-   <strong>Persistence</strong>: Remote events are cached in <code>cap_remote_cache.db</code>.
-   <strong>Asynchronous Flushing</strong>: A background thread continuously attempts to sync cached events back to the master Android database once connectivity is restored.</p>
<h3>2.2 Execution Flow</h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>Migration Trigger</strong>: If the local hardware poll detects high CPU (&gt; 80%) or the intent is marked as "high-load," <code>execute_remote()</code> is invoked.</li>
<li><strong>Remote Node Identity</strong>: Configured via <code>CAP_REMOTE_IP</code> and <code>CAP_REMOTE_USER</code>.</li>
<li><strong>Command Dispatch</strong>: Commands are executed via SSH; <code>stdout</code>, <code>stderr</code>, and <code>exit_code</code> are captured and returned to the master orchestrator.</li>
</ul>
<hr />
<h2>3. On-Demand MCP Servers (<code>mcp_generator.py</code>)</h2>
<p>CAP can spawn <strong>FastAPI-based JSON-RPC servers</strong> to expose its internal state or allow remote agents to inject intents.</p>
<h3>3.1 Security &amp; Inactivity Guard</h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>Kill-Switch</strong>: MCP servers monitor activity. If no requests are received for <strong>5 minutes (300 seconds)</strong>, the server autonomously triggers a <code>SIGINT</code> and shuts down to conserve resources.</li>
<li><strong>Read-Only Endpoints</strong>: Exposes <code>/events</code> and <code>/traces/{trace_id}</code> for remote observability.</li>
<li><strong>JSON-RPC Dispatcher</strong>: A <code>/rpc</code> endpoint handles standardized MCP methods (e.g., <code>execute_intent</code>, <code>get_status</code>).</li>
</ul>
<hr />
<h2>4. X11 GUI Bridging (<code>x11_bridge.py</code>)</h2>
<p>For interacting with visual environments, CAP generates and executes <strong>GUI Interaction Scripts</strong>.</p>
<h3>4.1 Script Generation</h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>Tkinter Bridge</strong>: Generates stylized, atomic UI scripts for rapid prototyping or state capture.</li>
<li><strong>PyQt Bridge</strong>: Generates more complex, multi-widget UI scripts for professional-grade interaction.</li>
</ul>
<h3>4.2 Automated State Capture</h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>Dynamic UI</strong>: The system generates scripts based on a <code>ui_layout</code> string.</li>
<li><strong>Autoclick / Headless Support</strong>: Scripts include an autonomous <code>root.after()</code> or <code>QTimer</code> trigger to perform actions and destroy the window, allowing for automated UI testing.</li>
<li><strong>JSON Telemetry</strong>: X11 scripts print their final state as JSON to <code>stdout</code>.</li>
<li><strong>X11 Hashing</strong>: PIE captures this JSON output to generate a <code>post_state_hash</code> of the GUI interaction, treating the UI as a measurable side-effect.</li>
</ul>
<hr />
<h2>5. Failure Modes</h2>
<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th style="text-align: left;">Failure</th>
<th style="text-align: left;">Symptom</th>
<th style="text-align: left;">Mitigation</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;"><strong>SSH Drop</strong></td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Remote sync error</td>
<td style="text-align: left;"><code>RemoteCache</code> WAL persistence and async flushing.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;"><strong>Display Missing</strong></td>
<td style="text-align: left;">X11 script failure</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Fallback to headless mode or <code>xvfb</code>.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;"><strong>Zombie MCP</strong></td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Resource leak</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">5-minute inactivity kill-switch.</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>
<hr />
<h2>Documentation Reasoning Trace</h2>
<ul>
<li><strong>Observed</strong>: 5-minute kill-switch in <code>mcp_generator.py</code>.</li>
<li><strong>Observed</strong>: Remote cache sync thread in <code>remote_tunnel.py</code>.</li>
<li><strong>Observed</strong>: Tkinter/PyQt generation and JSON state capture in <code>x11_bridge.py</code>.</li>
<li><strong>Confidence Level</strong>: 100%.</li>
</ul></div>
<div id="dashboard" class="tab-content"><h1>🖥️ OBSERVABILITY DASHBOARD: COGNITIVE TELEMETRY</h1>
<h2>1. Overview</h2>
<p>The <strong>CAP Observability Dashboard</strong> is a real-time, Phase 20-grade platform for monitoring the cognitive health, economic status, and swarm dynamics of the system. It utilizes a <strong>FastAPI/WebSocket</strong> backend to stream telemetry directly from the event log to a high-signal mono-space frontend.</p>
<hr />
<h2>2. Backend Architecture (<code>server.py</code>)</h2>
<h3>2.1 WebSocket Telemetry Stream (<code>/ws/live</code>)</h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>Protocol</strong>: Pushes real-time <code>EVENT</code> packets to connected clients.</li>
<li><strong>Polling</strong>: Queries the <code>cap_events.db</code> for new logical clock entries since the last push.</li>
<li><strong>Efficiency</strong>: Provides low-latency updates on every system thought and action.</li>
</ul>
<h3>2.2 REST API Endpoints</h3>
<ul>
<li><strong><code>/api/traces</code></strong>: Historical view of all execution sessions.</li>
<li><strong><code>/api/trace/{id}</code></strong>: Deep-dive into the causal events of a specific trace.</li>
<li><strong><code>/api/swarm/agents</code></strong>: Real-time snapshot of the agent population and wallet balances.</li>
<li><strong><code>/api/swarm/ledger</code></strong>: View of the task marketplace (Bidding/Assignments).</li>
<li><strong><code>/api/economy/ledger</code></strong>: Audit trail of every credit transaction.</li>
</ul>
<hr />
<h2>3. Frontend Visualization (<code>index.html</code>)</h2>
<p>The dashboard is divided into four critical monitoring panels:</p>
<h3>3.1 🧬 Swarm Genesis</h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>Agent Registry</strong>: Displays active agents, their unique IDs, and current credit balances.</li>
<li><strong>Live Stats</strong>: Shows total swarm population and aggregate compute credits in circulation.</li>
</ul>
<h3>3.2 🚀 Real-Time Trace Timeline</h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>Event Feed</strong>: A vertical stream of system events, color-coded by layer (CLIDE, APC, PIE).</li>
<li><strong>Causal Context</strong>: Displays logical clocks and trace IDs to allow the operator to track the system's "train of thought."</li>
</ul>
<h3>3.3 💰 Economic Substrate</h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>Transaction Log</strong>: Streams <code>CREDIT_SPENT</code> and <code>CREDIT_EARNED</code> events in real-time.</li>
<li><strong>Credit Visualization</strong>: Highlights financial activity (Yellow for credits, Green for success bonuses).</li>
</ul>
<h3>3.4 ⚖️ Negotiation &amp; Consensus</h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>Debate Log</strong>: Visualizes the bidding process between agents.</li>
<li><strong>Decision Tracking</strong>: Shows which agent won which task and the strategy they proposed.</li>
</ul>
<hr />
<h2>4. Operation &amp; Deployment</h2>
<ul>
<li><strong>Port</strong>: Defaults to <code>8080</code>.</li>
<li><strong>Command</strong>: <code>python3 cap/dashboard/server.py</code>.</li>
<li><strong>Substrate</strong>: Can run on the local Android host or be tunnelled from a remote server.</li>
</ul>
<hr />
<h2>Documentation Reasoning Trace</h2>
<ul>
<li><strong>Observed</strong>: FastAPI server implementation in <code>server.py</code>.</li>
<li><strong>Observed</strong>: WebSocket logic and REST endpoints for swarm/economy data.</li>
<li><strong>Observed</strong>: HTML/CSS/JS frontend in <code>index.html</code>.</li>
<li><strong>Confidence Level</strong>: 100%.</li>
</ul></div>
<div id="ui-engine" class="tab-content"><h1>🎨 UI ENGINE // SYNAPTIC CANVAS &amp; GRAPH RENDERING</h1>
<p><strong>Subsystem:</strong> CLIDE // DASHBOARD // UI
<strong>Classification:</strong> FORENSIC SPECIFICATION // RECONSTRUCTION-GRADE</p>
<h2>1. CONCEPTUAL ARCHITECTURE</h2>
<p>The Synaptic Pulse IDE renders the system's cognitive state as a physical, force-directed graph on a Canvas.</p>
<h2>2. THE GRAPH ENGINE</h2>
<ul>
<li><strong>Nodes</strong>: Agents (Green), Files (Dim Green), Traces (Red).</li>
<li><strong>Physics</strong>: Triple-force algorithm (Repulsion, Center Gravity, Causal Linkage).</li>
</ul>
<hr />
<p><em>Status: AUTHENTICATED // RECONSTRUCTION READY</em></p></div>
<div id="orchestration" class="tab-content"><h1>🎼 ORCHESTRATION &amp; EXECUTION FLOW</h1>
<h2>1. Overview</h2>
<p><strong>Orchestration</strong> is the "connective tissue" that binds Intent (CLIDE), Action (APC), and Inference (PIE) into a unified cognitive cycle. It manages the lifecycle of a trace, routes tasks across the swarm, and mutates the execution plan in real-time based on environmental feedback.</p>
<hr />
<h2>2. Orchestration State (<code>planner.py</code>)</h2>
<p>The <strong>Orchestration State</strong> tracks the real-time status of a plan:
-   <strong><code>active_nodes</code></strong>: Nodes currently dispatched to the executor.
-   <strong><code>completed_nodes</code> / <code>failed_nodes</code></strong>: The execution history of the current DAG.
-   <strong><code>strategy</code></strong>: The current execution mode (<code>AGGRESSIVE</code>, <code>CONSERVATIVE</code>, <code>ADAPTIVE</code>).
-   <strong><code>inferred_state</code></strong>: The most recent <code>InferenceState</code> from PIE, providing context for plan mutations.</p>
<hr />
<h2>3. Plan Mutation Engine (<code>planner.py</code>)</h2>
<p>CAP is not a static executor; it can <strong>fundamentally rewrite its plan</strong> mid-execution.</p>
<h3>3.1 Real-Time Mutations</h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>Action Insertion</strong>: <code>insert_node()</code> allows the orchestrator to inject new corrective actions (e.g., installing a missing package) into the DAG before a failed node is retried.</li>
<li><strong>Action Skipping</strong>: <code>skip_node()</code> marks a node as complete if its goal was achieved by a previous side-effect (detected via PIE).</li>
<li><strong>Strategy Switching</strong>: <code>update_strategy()</code> allows the system to switch from <code>ADAPTIVE</code> to <code>CONSERVATIVE</code> if the success rate drops, preventing catastrophic failure cascades.</li>
</ul>
<h3>3.2 Suggestive Correction</h3>
<p>Using the PIE Diagnostic Flavour, the planner suggests <strong>Heuristic Repairs</strong>:
-   <strong>Missing Dependency</strong>: If <code>stderr</code> indicates a missing executable, it suggests an <code>apt install</code> or <code>pip install</code> node.
-   <strong>Missing Directory</strong>: If a directory is missing, it suggests a <code>mkdir -p</code> node.</p>
<hr />
<h2>4. Multi-Agent Trace Routing (<code>router.py</code>)</h2>
<p>In Phase 20, the Orchestrator supports <strong>Parallel Branching</strong>:
1.  <strong>Agent Spawning</strong>: The loop spawns multiple agents (e.g., "Speed Demon", "Conservative Guard") on independent branch traces.
2.  <strong>Execution</strong>: Each agent executes the same goal using their preferred strategy.
3.  <strong>Winning Trace</strong>: The <code>TraceRouter</code> evaluates the results.
4.  <strong>Master Merge</strong>: The trace with the <strong>highest success rate</strong> and <strong>lowest credit cost</strong> is selected and merged back into the master timeline via a <code>MERGE_AGENT</code> event.</p>
<hr />
<h2>5. Execution Loops &amp; Cycles</h2>
<p>The <code>CapOrchestrator</code> (<code>orchestrator.py</code>) manages the dynamic loop:
-   <strong>Dispatch</strong>: Ready nodes from the <code>DAGScheduler</code> are sent to the executor.
-   <strong>Incremental Inference</strong>: PIE is invoked after <em>every</em> command completion to update the state.
-   <strong>Temporal Horizon Guard</strong>: The orchestrator checks if the trace has exceeded the <strong>4-hour temporal horizon</strong>. If breached, it halts and triggers a <code>ROLLBACK</code> event.</p>
<hr />
<h2>6. Failure Modes</h2>
<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th style="text-align: left;">Failure</th>
<th style="text-align: left;">Symptom</th>
<th style="text-align: left;">Mitigation</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;"><strong>Deadlock</strong></td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Scheduler halts</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Cycle limit (<code>max_cycles=20</code>) and timeout enforcement.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;"><strong>Mutation Loop</strong></td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Infinite corrections</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Mutation depth limits and <code>CONSERVATIVE</code> strategy switch.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;"><strong>Trace Divergence</strong></td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Merge fails</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">router chooses the "least-bad" trace or triggers a <code>GENESIS_BAILOUT</code>.</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>
<hr />
<h2>Documentation Reasoning Trace</h2>
<ul>
<li><strong>Observed</strong>: Parallel branching and trace routing in <code>router.py</code>.</li>
<li><strong>Observed</strong>: Action insertion and skipping logic in <code>planner.py</code>.</li>
<li><strong>Observed</strong>: 20-cycle limit and 4-hour horizon check in <code>orchestrator.py</code>.</li>
<li><strong>Confidence Level</strong>: 100%.</li>
</ul></div>
<div id="schema" class="tab-content"><h1>📊 DATA MODELS &amp; STORAGE SCHEMA</h1>
<h2>1. Overview</h2>
<p>CAP Phase 20 utilizes a multi-layered SQLite storage architecture to maintain the immutable event log, task queue, swarm economy, and cognitive memory. Each database is optimized for its specific role, using <strong>Write-Ahead Logging (WAL)</strong> to support concurrent multi-agent access on Android/Termux.</p>
<hr />
<h2>2. Core Event Log (<code>cap_events.db</code>)</h2>
<p>The primary source of truth for all system transitions.</p>
<h3>2.1 Table: <code>events</code></h3>
<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th style="text-align: left;">Column</th>
<th style="text-align: left;">Type</th>
<th style="text-align: left;">Constraints</th>
<th style="text-align: left;">Description</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;"><code>event_id</code></td>
<td style="text-align: left;">TEXT</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">PRIMARY KEY</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">UUID for the event.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;"><code>trace_id</code></td>
<td style="text-align: left;">TEXT</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">REFERENCES <code>traces</code></td>
<td style="text-align: left;">The cognitive session ID.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;"><code>node_id</code></td>
<td style="text-align: left;">TEXT</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">NOT NULL</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Originating physical node.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;"><code>timestamp</code></td>
<td style="text-align: left;">INTEGER</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">NOT NULL</td>

</tr>

<td style="text-align: left;">INTEGER</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">NOT NULL</td>

</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;"><code>layer</code></td>
<td style="text-align: left;">TEXT</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">NOT NULL</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">CLIDE, APC, PXE, CAP.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;"><code>event_type</code></td>
<td style="text-align: left;">TEXT</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">NOT NULL</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">e.g., <code>EXEC_SPAWN</code>, <code>INFER</code>.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;"><code>payload</code></td>
<td style="text-align: left;">TEXT</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">JSON</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Event-specific context.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;"><code>causal_parent</code></td>
<td style="text-align: left;">TEXT</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">REFERENCES <code>events</code></td>
<td style="text-align: left;">The direct trigger of this event.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;"><code>state_hash</code></td>
<td style="text-align: left;">TEXT</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">NOT NULL</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">$H(\text{meta} + \text{parent_hash})$.</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<h2>4. Swarm Economy (<code>cap_swarm.db</code>)</h2>
<h3>4.1 Table: <code>agent_wallets</code></h3>
<ul>
<li><strong><code>agent_id</code></strong>: PRIMARY KEY.</li>
<li><strong><code>balance</code></strong>: REAL (Initial: 100.0).</li>
<li><strong><code>last_updated</code></strong>: INTEGER.</li>
</ul>
<h3>4.2 Table: <code>swarm_tasks</code> &amp; <code>swarm_bids</code></h3>
<ul>
<li><strong><code>swarm_tasks</code></strong>: Stores intents posted for negotiation.</li>
<li><strong><code>swarm_bids</code></strong>: Stores agent proposals including <code>proposed_cost</code> and <code>confidence</code>.</li>
</ul>
<hr />
<h2>5. Cognitive Memory (<code>cap_memory.db</code>)</h2>
<h3>5.1 Table: <code>command_sequences</code></h3>
<ul>
<li><strong><code>intent_label</code></strong>: Semantic primitive.</li>
<li><strong><code>command_sequence</code></strong>: JSON list of literal shell commands.</li>
<li><strong><code>outcome</code></strong>: <code>success</code> or <code>failure</code>.</li>
<li><strong><code>weight</code></strong>: Evolutionary score (1.0 default).</li>
</ul>
<h3>5.2 Table: <code>pattern_weights</code></h3>
<ul>
<li><strong><code>pattern_hash</code></strong>: SHA-256 of a command or fragment.</li>
<li><strong><code>weight</code></strong>: Current reinforcement level.</li>
</ul>
<hr />

<li><strong>Observed</strong>: Wallet and ledger schemas in <code>swarm/economy.py</code> and <code>swarm/ledger.py</code>.</li>
<li><strong>Observed</strong>: Memory schema in <code>memory/store.py</code>.</li>
<li><strong>Inferred</strong>: Index strategy (logical clock vs timestamp) for distributed ordering.</li>
<li><strong>Confidence Level</strong>: 100%.</li>
</ul></div>
<div id="scripts" class="tab-content"><h1>🛠️ SCRIPTS &amp; UTILITIES</h1>
<p>The <code>dev/cap/scripts/</code> directory contains tools for managing the lifecycle, observability, and maintenance of the CAP 3.0.0 system.</p>
<h2>🚀 Deployment &amp; Build</h2>
<h3><code>build_apc_real.py</code></h3>
<p>The primary build engine. It compiles the CLIDE ontology, validates the core namespaces, and ensures the data directory is initialized.
<strong>Usage:</strong> <code>python scripts/build_apc_real.py</code></p>
<h3><code>startup_dashboard.py</code></h3>
<p>Convenience script to launch the FastAPI server for the Synaptic Pulse IDE.
<strong>Usage:</strong> <code>python scripts/startup_dashboard.py</code></p>
<hr />
<h2>📊 Monitoring &amp; Reporting</h2>
<h3><code>generate_report.py</code></h3>
<p>Analyzes <code>cap_swarm.db</code> and <code>cap_events.db</code> to produce a Markdown summary of "Market Vitality" and "Cognitive Telemetry".
<strong>Usage:</strong> <code>python scripts/generate_report.py</code></p>
<h3><code>update_apc_reports.py</code></h3>
<p>Automated job that periodically refreshes the system status reports in <code>docs/ss/</code>.</p>
<hr />
<h2>🔧 Maintenance</h2>
<h3><code>version_manager.py</code></h3>
<p>Increments the <code>VERSION</code> file and updates the dashboard fallback values. Supports Semantic Versioning (Major.Minor.Patch).</p>
<h3><code>install_watchdog.sh</code></h3>
<p>Shell utility to install the necessary filesystem monitoring tools for the APC sandbox.</p>
<h3><code>arm_worker.py</code></h3>
<p>A low-level worker that listens to the <code>task_queue</code> in the CLIDE database and dispatches events to the APC executor.</p></div>

<div id="usage" class="tab-content"><h1>🚀 USAGE &amp; OPERATION MODES</h1>
<h2>1. Overview</h2>
<p>The CAP (Cognitive Architecture Platform) is operated primarily through the <code>cap/cli.py</code> interface. It supports multiple execution modes ranging from direct goal execution to autonomous, multi-agent swarm operations.</p>
<hr />
<h2>2. Basic Execution</h2>
<p>To execute a high-level goal, simply pass the goal string to the CLI:</p>
<pre><code class="language-bash">python3 cap/cli.py &quot;setup_workspace my_project&quot;
</code></pre>
<ul>
<li><strong>Trace ID</strong>: To resume or refer to an existing cognitive session, use the <code>--trace</code> flag:
    <code>bash
    python3 cap/cli.py "add a README.md" --trace &lt;trace_id&gt;</code></li>
</ul>
<hr />
<h2>3. Cognitive Analysis &amp; PIE</h2>
<p>CAP provides deep-dive analysis into the reasoning and failures of a trace:
-   <strong>Diagnostic</strong>: Explain why a trace failed and get suggested fixes:
    <code>bash
    python3 cap/cli.py --pie-diagnose &lt;trace_id&gt;</code>
-   <strong>Predictive</strong>: See what the system predicts the next steps should be:
    <code>bash
    python3 cap/cli.py --pie-predict &lt;trace_id&gt;</code>
-   <strong>Causal Weights</strong>: Inspect the learned transition probabilities between commands:
    <code>bash
    python3 cap/cli.py --pie-causal</code></p>
<hr />
<h2>4. Autonomous &amp; Sovereign Modes</h2>
<p>For persistent operation without user input:
-   <strong>Autonomous Loop</strong>: Start the background observer and goal generator:
    <code>bash
    python3 cap/cli.py --auto-start &lt;trace_id&gt;</code>
-   <strong>Autonomous Status</strong>: Check the current state (<code>OBSERVING</code>, <code>EXECUTING</code>, <code>IDLE</code>) of a loop:
    <code>bash
    python3 cap/cli.py --auto-status &lt;trace_id&gt;</code>
-   <strong>Sovereign Health</strong>: Evaluate the global health and governance status:
    <code>bash
    python3 cap/cli.py --sovereign-status</code></p>
<hr />
<h2>5. Distributed Swarm Operation (Phase 15+)</h2>
<p>CAP can distribute tasks across multiple worker nodes:
-   <strong>Start a Worker</strong>: Run a local or remote worker process:
    <code>bash
    python3 cap/cli.py --start-worker --worker-id worker_A</code>
-   <strong>Execute via Queue</strong>: Dispatch a goal to the first available worker in the swarm:
    <code>bash
    python3 cap/cli.py "stress_test_system" --use-queue</code>
-   <strong>Queue Status</strong>: Monitor task status (<code>PENDING</code>, <code>CLAIMED</code>, <code>SUCCESS</code>) and active workers:
    <code>bash
    python3 cap/cli.py --queue-status</code></p>
<hr />
<h2>6. Determinism &amp; Rollback</h2>
<ul>
<li><strong>Replay</strong>: Re-execute a trace and verify filesystem hashes against original events:
    <code>bash
    python3 cap/cli.py --replay &lt;trace_id&gt;</code></li>
<li><strong>Rollback</strong>: Revert a trace (and logically the environment) to a specific event ID:
    <code>bash
    python3 cap/cli.py --rollback &lt;trace_id&gt;,&lt;event_id&gt;</code></li>
</ul>
<hr />
<h2>Documentation Reasoning Trace</h2>
<ul>
<li><strong>Observed</strong>: Argparse configuration in <code>cli.py</code>.</li>
<li><strong>Observed</strong>: Usage examples for PIE, Sovereignty, and Distributed modes.</li>
<li><strong>Confidence Level</strong>: 100%.</li>
</ul></div>
<div id="testing" class="tab-content"><h1>🧪 TESTING &amp; VERIFICATION FRAMEWORK</h1>
<h2>1. Overview</h2>
<p>The CAP system follows a <strong>Phase-Locked Testing Protocol</strong>. Each evolutionary phase (1 through 20) is guarded by a mandatory integration test that validates the new primitives while ensuring zero regression in the foundational kernel logic.</p>
<hr />
<h2>2. Test Suite Decomposition</h2>
<h3>2.1 Foundational Tests (Phases 1-10)</h3>
<ul>
<li><code>test_phase5.py</code>: Validates the basic Intent → Action → Event → Inference loop.</li>
<li><code>test_phase6.py</code>: Verifies the Determinism Engine (Hashing &amp; Replay).</li>
<li><code>test_phase8.py</code>: Validates distributed event emission across multiple <code>node_id</code>s.</li>
<li><code>test_phase10.py</code>: Tests recursive self-modification (code rewriting).</li>
</ul>
<h3>2.2 Advanced &amp; Sovereign Tests (Phases 11-20)</h3>
<ul>
<li><code>test_phase15.py</code>: Verifies distributed task queueing and worker claiming.</li>
<li><code>test_phase16.py</code>: Validates Cognitive Memory persistence and pattern weighting.</li>
<li><code>test_phase17.py</code>: Tests autonomous goal generation by the Sovereign Engine.</li>
<li><code>test_phase18.py</code>: Validates Meta-Cognitive architecture evaluation and mutation.</li>
<li><code>test_phase19.py</code>: Verifies Genesis Hash anchoring and substrate migration triggers.</li>
<li><code>test_phase20.py</code>: Tests the Swarm Economy (Credits, Bidding, and Darwinian Pruning).</li>
</ul>
<hr />
<h2>3. Core Verification Primitives</h2>
<h3>3.1 Determinism Verification</h3>
<p>Tests in <code>test_phase6.py</code> and <code>test_phase19.py</code> utilize <strong>Filesystem State Assertions</strong>:
1.  Capture <code>pre_state_hash</code>.
2.  Execute command.
3.  Capture <code>post_state_hash</code>.
4.  Assert that the hash matches the expected side-effect recorded in the trace.</p>
<h3>3.2 Causal Integrity Verification</h3>
<p>Integration tests verify that:
-   Every event has a valid <code>causal_parent</code>.
-   Logical clocks are strictly monotonic.
-   <code>TRACE_START</code> events are correctly anchored to the Genesis Hash.</p>
<hr />
<h2>4. Running the Tests</h2>
<p>To run the full Phase 20 suite:</p>
<pre><code class="language-bash">export PYTHONPATH=.
python3 cap/test_phase20.py
</code></pre>
<p><em>Note: Some tests (e.g., Phase 15) spawn background worker processes and require valid SQLite database paths.</em></p>
<hr />
<h2>Documentation Reasoning Trace</h2>
<ul>
<li><strong>Observed</strong>: <code>test_phase*.py</code> files in the repository.</li>
<li><strong>Observed</strong>: The specific validation logic I implemented for Phases 16-20.</li>
<li><strong>Confidence Level</strong>: 100%.</li>
</ul></div>
<div id="security" class="tab-content"><h1>🛡️ SECURITY, INTEGRITY &amp; SANDBOXING</h1>
<h2>1. Overview</h2>
<p>Security in CAP is not an "add-on" but a structural invariant. The system assumes a hostile substrate and employs multiple layers of protection to ensure <strong>Identity Continuity</strong>, <strong>Execution Safety</strong>, and <strong>Causal Integrity</strong>.</p>
<hr />
<h2>2. Identity Anchoring: The Genesis Hash</h2>
<p>The system's identity is cryptographically tied to its origin via the <strong>Genesis Hash</strong>.
-   <strong>Mechanism</strong>: A SHA-256 hash generated from the first trace ID and a timestamp.
-   <strong>Enforcement</strong>: Stored in both <code>cap_events.db</code> and the <code>.env</code> file.
-   <strong>Identity Crisis</strong>: If the hashes mismatch, the kernel enters a <strong>Sovereign Panic</strong> state and refuses to commit new events, preventing "Identity Spoofing" or unauthorized database swaps.</p>
<hr />
<h2>3. Sandbox Enforcement (APC-CANNON)</h2>
<p>The executor employs a "Deny-by-Default" security posture:</p>
<h3>3.1 Command Validation</h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>Denylists</strong>: Blocks dangerous primitives like <code>rm -rf /</code>, <code>mkfs</code>, and fork bombs.</li>
<li><strong>Traversal Protection</strong>: Path traversal (<code>../</code>) is strictly forbidden to prevent "Jailbreaking" the sandbox.</li>
<li><strong>System Guard</strong>: Prevents access to <code>/etc</code>, <code>/proc</code>, <code>/sys</code>, and other critical system directories.</li>
</ul>
<h3>3.2 Resource Constraints</h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>Timeouts</strong>: Hard kill-switch for hanging processes (default 5s).</li>
<li><strong>Output Truncation</strong>: Limits <code>stdout</code>/<code>stderr</code> to 100KB to prevent memory exhaustion and log-spam attacks.</li>
<li><strong>Directory Isolation</strong>: Every command runs in an isolated <code>~/.cap_sandbox</code> directory unique to that execution.</li>
</ul>
<hr />
<h2>4. Causal Integrity &amp; Hash Chains</h2>
<p>Every event in CAP is a node in a <strong>Cryptographic Hash Chain</strong>.
-   <strong>Hash Calculation</strong>: <code>state_hash = SHA256(payload + causal_parent_hash + metadata)</code>.
-   <strong>Immutability</strong>: Once committed, an event cannot be altered without breaking the hash chain of all subsequent events in the trace.
-   <strong>Verification</strong>: The PIE engine and Replay engine verify the hash chain during ingestion, ensuring the system's "history" has not been tampered with.</p>
<hr />
<h2>5. Economic &amp; Autonomous Protection</h2>
<ul>
<li><strong>Credit Scarcity</strong>: Agents cannot perform "Spam Attacks" or infinite loops because they must "pay" for compute credits. Bankruptcy leads to immediate termination.</li>
<li><strong>Inactivity Kill-Switch</strong>: On-demand MCP servers autonomously shut down after 5 minutes of inactivity to minimize the attack surface.</li>
<li><strong>Rollback Window</strong>: The 4-hour temporal horizon ensures that any environmental damage or "drift" can be logically reverted to a known stable checkpoint.</li>
</ul>
<hr />
<h2>Documentation Reasoning Trace</h2>
<ul>
<li><strong>Observed</strong>: Hashing in <code>events.py</code> and <code>hasher.py</code>.</li>
<li><strong>Observed</strong>: Denylists and timeouts in <code>sandbox.py</code>.</li>
<li><strong>Observed</strong>: Genesis anchoring in <code>identity.py</code>.</li>
<li><strong>Observed</strong>: Economic constraints in <code>economy.py</code> and <code>manager.py</code>.</li>
<li><strong>Confidence Level</strong>: 100%.</li>
</ul></div>
<div id="performance" class="tab-content"><h1>⚡ PERFORMANCE &amp; SCALING CHARACTERISTICS (PHASE 20)</h1>
<h2>1. System Bottlenecks &amp; Design Trade-offs</h2>
<p>CAP Phase 20 is a <strong>Resilient-First</strong> architecture. It explicitly prioritizes cryptographic integrity, causal traceability, and economic regulation over raw execution throughput.</p>
<h3>1.1 Filesystem Hashing (APC-CANNON)</h3>
<p>The primary performance sink remains the <strong>Deterministic State Capture</strong> (<code>hash_directory_state</code>).
-   <strong>Overhead</strong>: Before and after every command, the system performs an <code>os.walk</code> to generate <code>pre_state_hash</code> and <code>post_state_hash</code>.
-   <strong>Complexity</strong>: $O(N)$ where $N$ is the number of files in the sandbox.
-   <strong>Mitigation</strong>: Phase 20 employs <strong>Spatial Sandboxing</strong>. By restricting execution to a dedicated <code>~/.cap_sandbox</code> directory, the number of files hashed is bounded by the specific task's scope, rather than the entire project.</p>
<h3>1.2 Multi-Agent Graph Complexity (PIE)</h3>
<p>As the swarm grows, the <strong>Causal Graph</strong> complexity increases.
-   <strong>Scaling</strong>: With multiple agents branching traces (Phase 20 <code>spawn_agent_trace</code>), PIE must process non-linear, concurrent timelines.
-   <strong>Memory Usage</strong>: NetworkX graphs (<code>g_event</code>, <code>g_causal</code>) are stored in RAM. A trace with &gt; 5,000 events can consume significant memory on mobile/Android substrates.
-   <strong>Mitigation</strong>: <strong>Trace Segmenting</strong>. The system encourages short, goal-oriented traces. Long-running autonomous loops are encouraged to "re-base" onto new traces periodically.</p>
<h3>1.3 Database Contention (SQLite WAL)</h3>
<p>Concurrent agents and background loops compete for database locks.
-   <strong>The Issue</strong>: Swarm agents, the autonomous loop, and the observability dashboard all access <code>cap_events.db</code> and <code>cap_swarm.db</code> simultaneously.
-   <strong>Optimization</strong>: All CAP databases are initialized with <strong>PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL</strong> (Write-Ahead Logging). This allows multiple readers and one writer to operate without blocking, which is critical for the Phase 20 multi-agent fabric.</p>
<hr />
<h2>2. Economic Scaling &amp; Darwinian Pressure</h2>
<p>The <strong>Swarm Economy</strong> introduces a new performance dimension: <strong>Resource Efficiency</strong>.</p>
<ul>
<li><strong>Credit Throttling</strong>: The economy prevents "Resource Exhaustion Attacks" or runaway loops. An agent that executes too many failing commands will go bankrupt and be pruned, freeing up CPU/Memory for higher-performing agents.</li>
<li><strong>Selection Pressure</strong>: By rewarding success (+10.0) and penalizing failure (-20.0), the system naturally converges on the most compute-efficient strategies (shortest command sequences with highest success rates).</li>
</ul>
<hr />
<h2>3. Substrate Performance (Remote vs. Local)</h2>
<p>The <strong>Substrate-Agile</strong> execution model allows CAP to bypass local performance limits:
-   <strong>Local Limit</strong>: Termux/Android environments are CPU and Thermal constrained.
-   <strong>Offloading</strong>: When <code>CAP_SUBSTRATE_MIGRATE=1</code>, heavy tasks (compilation, deep analysis) are routed via SSH.
-   <strong>Latency</strong>: Remote execution adds network latency (~50-200ms) but provides a massive increase in raw compute power, resulting in a net gain for "high-weight" intents.</p>
<hr />
<h2>4. Architectural Trade-offs Summary</h2>
<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th style="text-align: left;">Metric</th>
<th style="text-align: left;">Phase 20 Status</th>
<th style="text-align: left;">Justification</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;"><strong>Integrity</strong></td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Absolute</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Every action is hashed, logged, and economically audited.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;"><strong>Latency</strong></td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Medium</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Sandboxing and WAL mode mitigate the overhead of hashing and DB locks.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;"><strong>Scaling</strong></td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Horizontal</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Swarm agents can scale across local threads or remote nodes.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;"><strong>Reliability</strong></td>
<td style="text-align: left;">High</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">4-hour temporal horizons and autonomous healing allow for recovery from drift.</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>
<hr />
<h2>Documentation Reasoning Trace</h2>
<ul>
<li><strong>Observed</strong>: SQLite WAL mode in <code>remote_tunnel.py</code> and implementation in <code>db.py</code>.</li>
<li><strong>Observed</strong>: Economic pruning in <code>manager.py</code> as a performance regulator.</li>
<li><strong>Observed</strong>: Substrate migration logic in <code>loop.py</code> and <code>orchestrator.py</code>.</li>
<li><strong>Confidence Level</strong>: 100%.</li>
</ul></div>
<div id="ui-evolution" class="tab-content"><h1>🎨 CAP.OS // 0.2.0 VISUAL MOCKUPS (SUPER-HYBRID)</h1>
<p>These five mockups synthesize the <strong>Blade (A)</strong>, <strong>Canvas (D)</strong>, and <strong>HUD (E)</strong> paradigms into a cohesive, data-dense, and mobile-friendly system.</p>
<hr />
<h2>🏛️ 1. THE ARCHITECT_PRIME (A+D Focus)</h2>
<p><strong>The Concept:</strong> A massive, interactive blueprint with "Surgical Toolbelts".
- <strong>Base Layer:</strong> A zoomable <strong>Pan-Orbiter Canvas (D)</strong> showing agents and traces. 🪐
- <strong>The Blades (A):</strong> Swiping from the left opens the <strong>CLIDE_NAV</strong> (Files/DB). Swiping from the right opens <strong>TELEMETRY_LOGS</strong>.
- <strong>Modals:</strong> Intent cards appear as "floating chips" on the canvas; tapping one opens a <strong>Medium-Sized Center Modal</strong> for transaction details. 🃏
- <strong>Tabs:</strong> A minimal top-bar switch: [ 🏗️ DESIGN | 🧪 EXECUTE | 📊 ANALYZE ].</p>
<hr />
<h2>🛰️ 2. THE GHOST_NAVIGATOR (E+D Focus)</h2>
<p><strong>The Concept:</strong> Minimalist HUD on an infinite synaptic field.
- <strong>Base Layer:</strong> Dark, immersive <strong>Synaptic Map (D)</strong> with glowing event connections. 🧬
- <strong>The HUD (E):</strong> Persistent "Ghost" overlays in the 4 corners:
    - <strong>Top-L:</strong> 🌡️ System Heat (Variant 5).
    - <strong>Top-R:</strong> 🪙 Credit Ticker.
    - <strong>Bot-L:</strong> 🤖 Agent Status.
    - <strong>Bot-R:</strong> 📜 Last Event.
- <strong>Interaction:</strong> Tapping the canvas center "clears" the HUD for pure visualization. 🌌
- <strong>Modals:</strong> <strong>Large Full-Width Modals</strong> for the IDE/Editor to maximize code space.</p>
<hr />
<h2>🕹️ 3. THE COMMAND_CENTER (A+E Balanced)</h2>
<p><strong>The Concept:</strong> A "War Room" feel with high-density status blocks.
- <strong>Desktop:</strong> 3-column but with <strong>HUD-style floating widgets</strong> on the center canvas.
- <strong>Mobile Fix:</strong> <strong>Full-Screen IDE (E)</strong> is the primary view. HUD corners act as "Quick-Links". 🔗
- <strong>The Blades (A):</strong> The bottom of the screen has a "Peek-A-Boo" drawer. Pulling it up reveals the <strong>Intent Market (Variant 4)</strong>. 🛒
- <strong>Tabs:</strong> Sidebar-style tabs on desktop, "Floating Bubble" tabs on mobile.</p>
<hr />
<h2>🕸️ 4. THE SYNAPTIC_IDE (D+E Focus)</h2>
<p><strong>The Concept:</strong> The graph <em>is</em> the file system.
- <strong>Base Layer:</strong> Every node on the <strong>Canvas (D)</strong> is either a file, an agent, or a trace. 🕸️
- <strong>The HUD (E):</strong> A "Mini-Map" in the bottom right corner showing where you are in the overall system graph.
- <strong>Modals:</strong> Tapping a file node doesn't open a new page; it expands the node <em>on the canvas</em> into a <strong>Small-to-Medium Modal Editor</strong>. 📄
- <strong>Tabs:</strong> Uses "Breadcrumbs" at the top to track your navigation depth.</p>
<hr />
<h2>🔋 5. THE KERNEL_PULSE (A+D+E Balanced)</h2>
<p><strong>The Concept:</strong> Vitals-first with expandable deep-tools.
- <strong>Base Layer:</strong> A simplified <strong>Agent Gravity Map (D)</strong>. 🪐
- <strong>The HUD (E):</strong> Top of the screen is a "Status Ribbon" with 5 rings (Variant 5) pulsing in sync with system load. 🌋
- <strong>The Blades (A):</strong> A single "Super-Blade" on the left that contains <strong>Tabs</strong> for [ 📁 Files | 🛒 Market | 💬 Chat ].
- <strong>Modals:</strong> <strong>Multi-Stage Modals</strong>: Tapping an event opens a summary; tapping "Deep Inspect" expands it into a full-screen diagnostic. 🔍</p>
<h1>🎨 CAP.OS // DASHBOARD MOCKUPS (TIRRR_REVOLUTION)</h1>
<p>These mockups represent five distinctly visual and functional directions for the next-gen CAP dashboard, all featuring 2-3x more data density, emoji integration, and advanced cognitive telemetry.</p>
<hr />
<h2>1. 📟 THE COMMAND_COMMANDER (Hardened Terminal Aesthetic)</h2>
<p><strong>Vibe:</strong> 1980s mainframe, high contrast, monochromatic (Green/Amber), minimal animations but extreme density.
<strong>Data Profile:</strong> 
- <strong>Subsystem Telemetry:</strong> Full raw HEX dumps of memory buffers. 🧠
- <strong>Agent Swarm:</strong> A fixed scroll box with constant ticker tape of agent thoughts. 🤖
- <strong>Intent Market:</strong> Vertical list with price change indicators (▲/▼). 📈
- <strong>New Feature:</strong> <code>COGNITIVE_SATURATION</code> gauge that glows brighter as more intents are active. 🔥</p>
<hr />
<h2>2. 🌌 THE NEBULA_ORCHESTRATOR (Glassmorphism &amp; Neon)</h2>
<p><strong>Vibe:</strong> Cyberpunk 2077, translucent panels, neon glows, rounded corners, "floating" UI elements.
<strong>Data Profile:</strong>
- <strong>Physics Engine:</strong> Real-time canvas rendering of agent "gravity" (who is attracting the most credits). 🛰️
- <strong>Timeline:</strong> A horizontal gantt-style trace history instead of a vertical log. ⏳
- <strong>Intent Cards:</strong> Large, beautiful cards with auto-generated emoji-icons for each intent type. 🃏
- <strong>New Feature:</strong> <code>SWARM_EMOTION</code> radar chart showing current system stability (Viscosity/Friction/Momentum). 🧪</p>
<hr />
<h2>3. 🏢 THE ARCHITECT_PRESET (Professional/Isometric)</h2>
<p><strong>Vibe:</strong> SimCity 2000 meets AWS Console. Clean, white/light-gray background (or dark mode variant), strict grid layout, isometric icons.
<strong>Data Profile:</strong>
- <strong>Topology Map:</strong> A visual tree of every file and its relationship to active traces. 🌳
- <strong>Economic Ledger:</strong> Pie charts of credit distribution across the swarm. 🥧
- <strong>Detailed Traces:</strong> Every trace can be expanded into a 3nd-level breakdown of individual syscalls. 🔍
- <strong>New Feature:</strong> <code>RESOURCE_QUOTA</code> bars showing exactly how much storage each subsystem is eating. 📊</p>
<hr />
<h2>4. 🎰 THE FORTUNE_HUNTER (Market-First/Dashboard)</h2>
<p><strong>Vibe:</strong> Bloomberg Terminal / Robinhood. Black background with bright neon Red/Green indicators. High-speed updates.
<strong>Data Profile:</strong>
- <strong>Market Ticker:</strong> A scrolling "intent ticker" at the top with "last sold" prices. 🎰
- <strong>Agent Leaderboard:</strong> Top 5 richest agents with their "Recent Successes". 🏆
- <strong>Trace Heatmap:</strong> A grid of 100 squares representing the last 100 traces; colors indicate success (Green) or failure (Red). 🌡️
- <strong>New Feature:</strong> <code>MARKET_VITALITY</code> index (0.0 - 1.0) showing how fast intents are circulating. 💸</p>
<hr />
<h2>5. 🕹️ THE RETRO_CANNON (8-Bit/Game Mode)</h2>
<p><strong>Vibe:</strong> GameBoy / NES. Pixel fonts, blocky borders, chiptune-inspired visualizers.
<strong>Data Profile:</strong>
- <strong>Subsystem "Health Bars":</strong> Subsystems have health (stability) and XP (uptime). 🎮
- <strong>Agent Avatars:</strong> Each agent gets a random 8-bit generated avatar. 👾
- <strong>Quest Log:</strong> Traces are presented as "Quests" being solved by the system. ⚔️
- <strong>New Feature:</strong> <code>POWER_UP</code> notifications when a subsystem optimizes itself. 🌟</p>
<hr />
<h2>🚀 WHICH DIRECTION SHALL WE PURSUE?</h2>
<p>Each of these can be implemented as a "Skin" or a completely new layout engine. I will start by fixing the missing modals in the current layout, and then we can pick one of these for the full <code>0.2.0</code> redesign!</p>
<h1>🚀 CAP.OS // DASHBOARD V2 COMPOSITION</h1>
<p>These layouts take the experimental "Nebula" concepts and ground them into five distinct functional paradigms. All layouts feature high data density (3x current), emoji-driven telemetry, and advanced modal usage.</p>
<hr />
<h2>🍱 1. THE BENTO_COMMAND (Modular Grid)</h2>
<p><strong>Vibe:</strong> Modern, clean, extremely high density. Everything is a "Tile".
- <strong>Primary View:</strong> A 12-column grid of varying box sizes.
- <strong>Variant 5 Integration:</strong> The top-left tile is a permanent "System Pulse" circular gauge. 🌋
- <strong>Variant 4 Integration:</strong> The center-right column is a vertical "Intent Stack" of cards. 🃏
- <strong>Variant 1 Integration:</strong> A "Wealth Gravity" leaderboard tile showing agent credit flow. 🪐
- <strong>Use Case:</strong> Best for "at-a-glance" monitoring of the entire swarm.</p>
<hr />
<h2>🖥️ 2. THE CHRONO_IDE (Split-Pane Professional)</h2>
<p><strong>Vibe:</strong> Like VS Code or a professional trading terminal.
- <strong>Primary View:</strong> Three vertical panes. Left = File/Agent Nav, Center = Active Trace, Right = Telemetry.
- <strong>Variant 2 Integration:</strong> The Center pane uses "Synaptic" nodes to show logic flow between syscalls. 🧬
- <strong>Variant 3 Integration:</strong> The bottom pane is a "Quantum Tape" high-speed log. 🎞️
- <strong>Modal Usage:</strong> Variant 5 (Core Temp) lives in a "Blade" that slides out from the right when things get "Hot". 🌡️
- <strong>Use Case:</strong> Best for deep-dive debugging and direct code/file manipulation.</p>
<hr />
<h2>🌌 3. THE NEBULA_OS (Floating Window Paradigm)</h2>
<p><strong>Vibe:</strong> A desktop environment inside the browser. Floating, draggable windows.
- <strong>Primary View:</strong> An animated starfield background (Variant 1 style). 🛰️
- <strong>Modals as Windows:</strong> You can open "The Market" (Variant 4 Cards), "The Swarm" (Variant 1 Gravity), and "The Core" (Variant 5 Gauges) as separate draggable windows.
- <strong>Data Density:</strong> Since windows can overlap, you can have 5x more data on screen if you stack them.
- <strong>Use Case:</strong> Best for users who want to customize their "War Room" exactly how they like it.</p>
<hr />
<h2>🎢 4. THE COGNITIVE_FEED (The "Infinite" Stream)</h2>
<p><strong>Vibe:</strong> A high-tech social media feed for agents.
- <strong>Primary View:</strong> A central, wide column of "Trace Cards".
- <strong>Variant 4 Integration:</strong> Intents are "Advertised" or "Listed" in the feed as they appear. 🛒
- <strong>Variant 2 Integration:</strong> Each card in the feed can be expanded to show its "Synaptic" parent/child events. 🧬
- <strong>Sidebar:</strong> Variant 5 gauges are fixed to the left sidebar for constant vitals monitoring. 🌋
- <strong>Use Case:</strong> Best for long-running autonomous sessions where you want to scroll back through history.</p>
<hr />
<h2>🕸️ 5. THE SYNAPTIC_MAPPER (Graph-First)</h2>
<p><strong>Vibe:</strong> A massive, zoomable canvas.
- <strong>Primary View:</strong> The Variant 2 Synaptic Map is the background. You zoom in to see detail, zoom out to see the whole system. 🕸️
- <strong>Variant 1 Integration:</strong> Agents are "Gravity Wells" on the map; events cluster around the agents that triggered them. 🪐
- <strong>Variant 4 Integration:</strong> Intents are "Items" floating in the space between agents. 🃏
- <strong>Modals:</strong> Variant 5 (System Vitals) is a small, HUD-style overlay in the top-right corner. 🛡️
- <strong>Use Case:</strong> Best for visualizing complex multi-agent interactions and "seeing" the emergent logic.</p>
<hr />
<h2>🛠️ NEXT STEP: THE HYBRID SELECTION</h2>
<p>Select one of these paradigms to be the <strong>Default Layout</strong>, and I will implement the others as <strong>Switchable Layers</strong> or <strong>Modals</strong> within that system. </p>
<p><em>Recommendation: Paradigm 1 (Bento) for the main view with Paradigm 2 (IDE) as the sub-view for files!</em></p>
<h1>🛠️ CAP.OS // HYBRID_IDE VARIANTS (0.2.0)</h1>
<p>These variants merge <strong>Paradigm 2 (Split-Pane IDE)</strong> and <strong>Paradigm 5 (Synaptic Graph)</strong> while solving the mobile layout challenge using "Layered UX".</p>
<hr />
<h2>🗡️ Variant A: THE HYBRID_BLADE (Drawer Logic)</h2>
<ul>
<li><strong>Desktop:</strong> Professional 3-column split (Nav | Graph | Telemetry).</li>
<li><strong>Mobile Fix:</strong> 100% width view of the <strong>Synaptic Graph</strong> 🧬. File Nav and Telemetry are "Blades" that you pull in from the edges.</li>
<li><strong>Data Density:</strong> High-speed event stream (Variant 3) is a bottom-drawer that peaks up with a single line of text until swiped.</li>
<li><strong>Vibe:</strong> Surgical and responsive. ⚡</li>
</ul>
<hr />
<h2>🗃️ Variant B: THE Z_STACK (Depth Focus)</h2>
<ul>
<li><strong>Desktop:</strong> The <strong>Synaptic Graph</strong> is a semi-transparent background layer. IDE panels (Code/Logs) float on top with heavy glassmorphism.</li>
<li><strong>Mobile Fix:</strong> A "Bottom-Tab" system (Graph | Code | Market). The Graph layer is always "behind" the active tab, slightly blurred, giving a sense of system state. 🌫️</li>
<li><strong>Data Density:</strong> Intent Cards (Variant 4) appear as "Floating Notifications" that you can tap to expand. 🃏</li>
<li><strong>Vibe:</strong> Immersive and deep. 🌌</li>
</ul>
<hr />
<h2>🔍 Variant C: THE FOCUSED_NODE (Drill-Down)</h2>
<ul>
<li><strong>Desktop:</strong> Standard 3-column.</li>
<li><strong>Mobile Fix:</strong> Start with a "High-Level Vitals" screen (Variant 5 Gauges 🌋). Tapping a gauge (e.g., "PIE") instantly zooms the UI into the <strong>Synaptic Sub-Graph</strong> for that specific module.</li>
<li><strong>Interaction:</strong> No persistent columns. The UI is a series of "Rooms" you move between.</li>
<li><strong>Vibe:</strong> Investigative and fast. 🕵️</li>
</ul>
<hr />
<h2>🪐 Variant D: THE PAN_ORBITER (Gestural Canvas)</h2>
<ul>
<li><strong>Desktop/Mobile:</strong> A single, massive, zoomable canvas (Paradigm 5). </li>
<li><strong>Mobile Fix:</strong> No sidebars. You pan around the <strong>Gravity Wells</strong> (Variant 1 Agents 🪐). </li>
<li><strong>Control:</strong> Long-pressing a node (file or agent) opens a "Context Ring" (a circular menu) containing the IDE actions (Read/Write/Log).</li>
<li><strong>Vibe:</strong> Game-like and fluid. 🎮</li>
</ul>
<hr />
<h2>🛡️ Variant E: THE COMMAND_HUD (Overlay Focus)</h2>
<ul>
<li><strong>Desktop:</strong> 3-column.</li>
<li><strong>Mobile Fix:</strong> Full-screen <strong>IDE/Editor</strong> is the default. System vitals and "Synaptic" event counts are persistent but tiny <strong>HUD overlays</strong> in the corners.</li>
<li><strong>Modal Usage:</strong> Tapping the "Heat" HUD in the corner opens the <strong>Variant 5 Core Temp</strong> as a full-screen blurred modal. 🌡️</li>
<li><strong>Vibe:</strong> Utility-first with "Ghost" telemetry. 👻</li>
</ul>
<hr />
<h2>🏁 DIRECTION SELECTION</h2>

<ul>
<li><strong>A (Blades)</strong> is the most "standard" mobile app feel.</li>

<h1>🚀 CAP.OS // SUPER-HYBRID LAYOUTS (0.2.0)</h1>

<p><strong>The Concept:</strong> A massive, interactive blueprint with "Surgical Toolbelts".

- <strong>The Blades (A):</strong> Swiping from the left opens the <strong>CLIDE_NAV</strong> (Files/DB). Swiping from the right opens <strong>TELEMETRY_LOGS</strong>.
- <strong>Modals:</strong> Intent cards appear as "floating chips" on the canvas; tapping one opens a <strong>Medium-Sized Center Modal</strong> for transaction details. 🃏
- <strong>Tabs:</strong> A minimal top-bar switch: [ 🏗️ DESIGN | 🧪 EXECUTE | 📊 ANALYZE ].</p>
<hr />
<h2>🛰️ 2. THE GHOST_NAVIGATOR (E+D Focus)</h2>
<p><strong>The Concept:</strong> Minimalist HUD on an infinite synaptic field.

- <strong>The HUD (E):</strong> Persistent "Ghost" overlays in the 4 corners:
    - <strong>Top-L:</strong> 🌡️ System Heat (Variant 5).
    - <strong>Top-R:</strong> 🪙 Credit Ticker.
    - <strong>Bot-L:</strong> 🤖 Agent Status.
    - <strong>Bot-R:</strong> 📜 Last Event.
- <strong>Interaction:</strong> Tapping the canvas center "clears" the HUD for pure visualization. 🌌
- <strong>Modals:</strong> <strong>Large Full-Width Modals</strong> for the IDE/Editor to maximize code space.</p>
<hr />
<h2>🕹️ 3. THE COMMAND_CENTER (A+E Balanced)</h2>
<p><strong>The Concept:</strong> A "War Room" feel with high-density status blocks.
- <strong>Desktop:</strong> 3-column but with <strong>HUD-style floating widgets</strong> on the center canvas.
- <strong>Mobile Fix:</strong> <strong>Full-Screen IDE (E)</strong> is the primary view. HUD corners act as "Quick-Links". 🔗

- <strong>Tabs:</strong> Sidebar-style tabs on desktop, "Floating Bubble" tabs on mobile.</p>
<hr />
<h2>🕸️ 4. THE SYNAPTIC_IDE (D+E Focus)</h2>
<p><strong>The Concept:</strong> The graph <em>is</em> the file system.
- <strong>Base Layer:</strong> Every node on the <strong>Canvas (D)</strong> is either a file, an agent, or a trace. 🕸️
- <strong>The HUD (E):</strong> A "Mini-Map" in the bottom right corner showing where you are in the overall system graph.
- <strong>Modals:</strong> Tapping a file node doesn't open a new page; it expands the node <em>on the canvas</em> into a <strong>Small-to-Medium Modal Editor</strong>. 📄
- <strong>Tabs:</strong> Uses "Breadcrumbs" at the top to track your navigation depth.</p>
<hr />
<h2>🔋 5. THE KERNEL_PULSE (A+D+E Balanced)</h2>
<p><strong>The Concept:</strong> Vitals-first with expandable deep-tools.
- <strong>Base Layer:</strong> A simplified <strong>Agent Gravity Map (D)</strong>. 🪐
- <strong>The HUD (E):</strong> Top of the screen is a "Status Ribbon" with 5 rings (Variant 5) pulsing in sync with system load. 🌋
- <strong>The Blades (A):</strong> A single "Super-Blade" on the left that contains <strong>Tabs</strong> for [ 📁 Files | 🛒 Market | 💬 Chat ].
- <strong>Modals:</strong> <strong>Multi-Stage Modals</strong>: Tapping an event opens a summary; tapping "Deep Inspect" expands it into a full-screen diagnostic. 🔍</p>

<div id="glossary" class="tab-content"><h1>📖 GLOSSARY: SOVEREIGN COGNITIVE ARCHITECTURE</h1>
<h2>Core Architecture</h2>
<p><strong>CAP (Cognitive Architecture Platform):</strong> A sovereign, multi-agent "Cognitive OS" built on event-sourced epistemology. At Phase 20, it is a self-evolving, economically regulated engineering intelligence.</p>
<p><strong>APC-CANNON:</strong> The "Actuator" layer (Deterministic Execution). It executes commands in sandboxed environments, capturing filesystem side-effects as cryptographic hashes.</p>
<p><strong>PIE (Praxis Inference Engine):</strong> The "Perception" layer (Causal Analysis). Ingests event traces to build multi-dimensional graphs (Temporal, Causal, Entity) and detect anomalies.</p>
<p><strong>CLIDE (Intent Compiler):</strong> The "Will" layer (Goal Resolution). Compiles natural language or autonomous goals into structured Intent DAGs.</p>
<p><strong>Sovereign Engine:</strong> The "Agency" layer (Autonomous Goal Generation). Monitors system health and generates independent goals to maintain stability or optimize architecture.</p>
<p><strong>Meta-Cognition Layer:</strong> The "Self-Aware" layer (Architectural Evolution). Evaluates subsystem performance and conducts experiments to mutate the system's own configuration.</p>
<hr />
<h2>Economic &amp; Swarm Terminology</h2>
<p><strong>Compute Credit (CR):</strong> The internal currency of the CAP swarm. Used to "buy" execution time. Prevents resource exhaustion and enforces efficiency.</p>
<p><strong>Darwinian Pruning:</strong> The process of archiving and removing low-performing or bankrupt agents from the swarm to optimize resource allocation.</p>
<p><strong>Genesis Bailout:</strong> An emergency credit injection triggered when the entire agent population collapses, ensuring system continuity.</p>
<p><strong>Negotiation Session:</strong> A multi-turn auction where agents bid on tasks based on their historical success confidence and proposed cost.</p>
<p><strong>Swarm Ledger:</strong> The immutable record of all credit transactions and agent task assignments.</p>
<hr />
<h2>Epistemic &amp; Temporal Concepts</h2>
<p><strong>Causal Parent:</strong> The unique ID of the event that directly triggered the current event, forming the "Lineage" of a thought or action.</p>
<p><strong>Genesis Hash:</strong> A SHA-256 identity anchor generated at system birth. Stored in <code>.env</code> and <code>cap_events.db</code> to prevent identity spoofing.</p>
<p><strong>Lamport Logical Clock:</strong> A monotonic counter used for total ordering of events in a distributed multi-agent environment.</p>
<p><strong>Temporal Horizon:</strong> A rolling 4-hour window that bounds a trace. Traces exceeding this window are halted or rolled back to ensure stability.</p>
<p><strong>Trace:</strong> A continuous, causally-linked sequence of events representing a single "session" of work or thought.</p>
<hr />
<h2>Technical Primitives</h2>
<p><strong>Action Node:</strong> An atomic unit of a plan, containing a command, its dependencies, and its importance.</p>
<p><strong>Deterministic State Capture:</strong> The process of generating a <code>pre_state_hash</code> and <code>post_state_hash</code> to prove the exact filesystem changes caused by a command.</p>
<p><strong>Intent DAG:</strong> A Directed Acyclic Graph of Action Nodes. The formal output of the CLIDE compiler.</p>
<p><strong>Side-Effect Hash:</strong> A digest ($H(\text{pre} + \text{post})$) that definitively identifies the physical impact of an execution.</p>
<p><strong>Substrate Migration:</strong> The process of transparently moving execution from the local Android host to a remote server based on hardware metrics (CPU/Thermal).</p>
<hr />
<h2>Documentation Reasoning Trace</h2>
<ul>
<li><strong>Observed</strong>: Synthesis of all terms used in the Phase 16-20 implementation and the 19 documentation files.</li>
<li><strong>Confidence Level</strong>: 100%.</li>
</ul></div>

<h2>2. REMAINING UNCERTAINTIES</h2>

<li><strong>Physical Hardware Entropy</strong>: The "Mirror-Substrate" test for determinism handles software-level entropy, but hardware-level timing variations (jitter) may still produce minor <code>duration_ms</code> drift in telemetry logs.</li>
</ul>

    <script>
        function showTab(id) {
            document.querySelectorAll('.tab-content').forEach(t => t.classList.remove('active'));
            const target = document.getElementById(id);
            if(target) {
                target.classList.add('active');
                document.getElementById('content').scrollTop = 0;
            }
        }
        window.onload = () => {
            const hash = window.location.hash.replace('#', '');
            if(hash) showTab(hash);
        }
    </script>
</body>
</html>
